description:
You are a cloud-architect in the middle of migrating several legacy services to a new cluster.  
To standardise configuration handling you have decided to switch every service over to dotenv-based
environment management.  
Create a minimal, self-contained proof-of-concept in your terminal session that does the following:

1. Create a directory  
   /home/user/deployment

2. Inside that directory create a dotenv file named  
   /home/user/deployment/.env  
   with **exactly** three key/value pairs, each on its own line and in the precise order shown below.  
   Do not surround values with quotes and do not add extra whitespace.  

        AWS_REGION=us-east-1
        DB_HOST=10.10.10.7
        APP_MODE=staging

3. Still inside /home/user/deployment, add a tiny helper script called  
   /home/user/deployment/load_env.sh  
   that will read the .env file and export every variable found inside it into the current shell
   (the classic “`set -a ; . ./.env ; set +a`” technique is fine).  
   Make the script executable.

4. Run that helper script from the terminal so the three variables become part of the current
   session’s environment, then append a single verification block to a log file located at  
   /home/user/deployment/env_validation.log  

   The verification block must consist of **exactly** three lines, written in the order shown,
   **without** any leading or trailing whitespace, and each ending with a UNIX newline (`\n`):

        AWS_REGION=us-east-1
        DB_HOST=10.10.10.7
        APP_MODE=staging

   (The log file may already exist or not; your commands must ensure that after completion the file
   exists and ends with those three lines. If the file existed beforehand, simply append the new
   block so the required three lines appear at the very end.)

The automated checker will confirm:

• The directory /home/user/deployment exists.  
• The file /home/user/deployment/.env exists and contains exactly the three lines shown above,
  in that order, with newline characters separating them and with no additional lines, spaces or
  comments.  
• The script /home/user/deployment/load_env.sh exists, is executable, and *sourcing* it in a
  POSIX shell makes the three variables available to that shell.  
• The last three lines of /home/user/deployment/env_validation.log are, in order:  

        AWS_REGION=us-east-1
        DB_HOST=10.10.10.7
        APP_MODE=staging

No other side-effects, files or output are required. Completing the above steps should take no more
than three or four straightforward shell commands.
truth:
Expected artefacts after successful task completion:

1. Directory:
   /home/user/deployment          (permissions 755 or stricter)

2. File:
/home/user/deployment/.env  
Content (including trailing newline after the last line):

AWS_REGION=us-east-1\n
DB_HOST=10.10.10.7\n
APP_MODE=staging\n

File permissions: readable by user (600-644).

3. Script:
/home/user/deployment/load_env.sh  
Expected minimal functional content (exact byte-for-byte match **not** enforced, but script must
work):

#!/bin/sh
set -a
. "$(dirname "$0")/.env"
set +a

File must be executable (mode 700-755).

4. Log file:
/home/user/deployment/env_validation.log  
Its final three lines (i.e., tail -n 3 env_validation.log) must be exactly:

AWS_REGION=us-east-1\n
DB_HOST=10.10.10.7\n
APP_MODE=staging\n

The checker will:
• cat the .env file and compare to the target string above.
• source load_env.sh inside a fresh shell, then test:
      [ "$AWS_REGION" = "us-east-1" ] && \
      [ "$DB_HOST" = "10.10.10.7" ] && \
      [ "$APP_MODE" = "staging" ]
• tail -n 3 env_validation.log and compare to the three verification lines.

No additional files, directories, or environment variables are expected.
